title: Threshold Splits
description: See how feature thresholds divide data into left and right groups.
takeaways: A threshold split divides samples using feature <= threshold.
Different thresholds create different left and right groups.
Each split changes the weighted Gini score.

practiceLab.objective: Build candidate Age thresholds from a small patient table and split the patients with one midpoint threshold.
instructions: Run the first code cell to create the patients DataFrame as df and print it.
Identify which feature the lab uses for thresholds (Age).
Complete Exercise 1 by setting ages = df["Age"] and printing ages.
Complete Exercise 2 by setting unique_ages = sorted(df["Age"].unique()) and printing the result.
Complete Exercise 3 by filling the midpoint formula (unique_ages[i] + unique_ages[i + 1]) / 2 inside the loop.
Confirm that thresholds prints the midpoints between consecutive unique ages.
Complete Exercise 4 by creating left_group with Age <= 51.5 and right_group with Age > 51.5.
Run the cell that prints len(left_group) and len(right_group) and note each group size.
Optional: run the visualization cell to scatter Age vs Resting BP with the threshold line.
hints: Access a DataFrame column with df["Age"].
Use sorted(df["Age"].unique()) so each distinct age appears once in ascending order.
Boolean masks like df[df["Age"] <= threshold] select the left child rows.
practiceLab.expectedOutput: unique_ages equal to [42, 45, 48, 50, 53, 55, 58, 60, 63, 67]; thresholds equal to [43.5, 46.5, 49.0, 51.5, 54.0, 56.5, 59.0, 61.5, 65.0]; left/right groups created with threshold 51.5 and their printed lengths.
